title: “Monks, if that monk, while attending to a different wholesome sign, still has evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arising, then that…
content: “Monks, if that monk, while attending to a different wholesome sign, still has evil, unwholesome thoughts connected with desire, ill-will, and delusion arising, then that monk should examine the peril in those thoughts: ‘These thoughts are unwholesome, these thoughts are blameworthy, these thoughts lead to painful results.’ By examining the peril in those thoughts, those evil, unwholesome thoughts are abandoned and subside. With their abandonment, the mind becomes steadied, settled, unified, and concentrated.”

title: “Māgandiya, suppose a leprous person with sores and blisters on his limbs, being devoured by worms, scratching the openings of his wounds with his nails,…
content: “Māgandiya, suppose a leprous person with sores and blisters on his limbs, being devoured by worms, scratching the openings of his wounds with his nails, cauterizing his body over a charcoal pit. The more he scratches the wound openings and cauterizes his body, the more those wound openings become impure, foul-smelling, and infected, yet he feels some ease and satisfaction because of the itching of the wound openings. So too, Māgandiya, beings who have not abandoned lust for sensual pleasures, being consumed by craving for sensual pleasures, burned by the fever of sensual pleasures, indulge in sensual pleasures. The more they indulge in sensual pleasures, the more their craving for sensual pleasures increases, and the more they are burned by the fever of sensual pleasures, yet they feel some ease and satisfaction dependent on the five strands of sensual pleasure.”

title: “Here, these evil, unwholesome intentions of sensual desire, ill-will, and agitation are a hindrance for the noble disciple in training. Here, monks, a noble disciple…
content: “Here, these evil, unwholesome intentions of sensual desire, ill-will, and agitation are a hindrance for the noble disciple in training. Here, monks, a noble disciple reflects: ‘Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future — both belong to Māra’s realm, Māra’s domain, Māra’s bait, Māra’s food. Suppose I dwell with a mind that is expansive, great, having conquered the world and established the mind’s position. When I dwell with a mind that is expansive, great, having conquered the world and established the mind’s position, then those evil, unwholesome intentions of sensual desire, ill-will, and agitation will not arise; with their abandonment, my mind does not become confined, but rather immeasurable and well-developed.’”

title: “Householder, suppose there is a pit of glowing embers, deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing embers that are neither flaming nor smoking. Then…
content: “Householder, suppose there is a pit of glowing embers, deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing embers that are neither flaming nor smoking. Then a man comes who desires to live, not to die, who desires happiness and loathes suffering. Two strong men grab him by both arms and drag him toward that pit of embers. What do you think, householder? Would that man twist his body this way and that?” “Yes, venerable sir. Why is that? Because he knows: ‘If I fall into this pit of embers, I will thereby meet death or deadly suffering.’” “So too, householder, a noble disciple reflects: ‘The Blessed One has said that sensual pleasures are like a pit of embers — of much suffering, much despair, and the peril is even greater.’ Having seen this as it actually is with perfect wisdom … he cultivates such equanimity.”

title: “Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple reflects: ‘Now I am being overwhelmed by form. In the past, I was also overwhelmed by form just as…
content: “Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple reflects: ‘Now I am being overwhelmed by form. In the past, I was also overwhelmed by form just as I am now being overwhelmed by present form. If I were to delight in future form, then in the future I would be overwhelmed by form just as I am now being overwhelmed by present form.’ Reflecting thus, he has no regret for past form, no delight in future form, and practices disenchantment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present form.”

title: “Monks, if one does not intend, does not plan but still has a tendency towards something, that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness.…
content: “Monks, if one does not intend, does not plan but still has a tendency towards something, that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness. When there is a basis, there is an establishment of consciousness. When consciousness is established and grows, there is the descent of name-and-form. With name-and-form as condition, the six sense-bases … such is the origin of this whole mass of suffering.”

title: “Monks, if anyone says: ‘Māra’s complete snare’ — he would speak rightly of women. Indeed, women are ‘Māra’s complete snare.’”
content: “Monks, if anyone says: ‘Māra’s complete snare’ — he would speak rightly of women. Indeed, women are ‘Māra’s complete snare.’”

title: “Monks, the large deep lake refers to sensual pleasures. Monks, the great herd of deer refers to beings. Monks, the man who does not desire…
content: “Monks, the large deep lake refers to sensual pleasures. Monks, the great herd of deer refers to beings. Monks, the man who does not desire their welfare, benefit, security refers to Māra the Evil One. Monks, the dangerous path refers to the wrong eightfold path – wrong view, wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong concentration. Monks, the male decoy refers to delight and greed. Monks, the female decoy refers to ignorance. Monks, the man who desires their welfare, benefit, security refers to the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. The safe, secure path leading to happiness refers to the Noble Eightfold Path – right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.”

title: Elder Eraka: “O Eraka, painful are sensual pleasures! O Eraka, not happiness are sensual pleasures! One who desires sensual pleasures, desires suffering. One who does…
content:
Elder Eraka:
“O Eraka, painful are sensual pleasures!
O Eraka, not happiness are sensual pleasures!
One who desires sensual pleasures, desires suffering.
One who does not desire sensual pleasures, does not desire suffering.
O Eraka, know this as it is.”

title: - “What do you think, Mālukyaputta? Regarding forms cognizable by the eye that you have not seen, never saw before, do not see now, and…
content:
- “What do you think, Mālukyaputta? Regarding forms cognizable by the eye that you have not seen, never saw before, do not see now, and do not wish to see — do you have any desire, lust, or craving for them?” - “No, venerable sir.” - “Regarding sounds cognizable by the ear … odors cognizable by the nose … tastes cognizable by the tongue … touches cognizable by the body that you have not felt, never felt before, do not feel now, and do not wish to feel — do you have any desire, lust, or craving for them?” - “No, venerable sir.”

Seeing a form, if one’s mind is infatuated,
Attending to a pleasing sign,
The mind is attached to the feeling,
Clinging to the form remains.
His feelings increase,
Manyfold, born from form —
Greed and hurtful thoughts
Harm his mind.
Thus accumulating suffering,
He is said to be far from Nibbāna.

Hearing a sound, mind infatuated …
Smelling an odor …
Tasting a flavor …
If one’s mind is infatuated,
Attending to a pleasing sign,
The mind attached to the feeling,
Clinging to the flavor remains.
His feelings increase,
Manyfold, born from flavor —
Greed and hurtful thoughts
Harm his mind.
Thus accumulating suffering,
He is said to be far from Nibbāna.
